topic: wet season approaching — stocking up supplies

turns.content: The build-up has started. Humidity is climbing and the clouds stack up every afternoon now. I need to get the wet season supplies sorted before the roads close. Help me think through what I need.
Good to start planning early. How long are you typically cut off during the wet season, and is this just for your household or does the ranger station need stocking separately?

Both. The house and the station. Roads can be cut for 4 to 6 weeks, sometimes longer if the river stays up. There is me and the two kids at home. I keep home supplies separate from the station because sometimes you cannot even get to the station when the water is across the road.

That's smart to keep them separate. For a household of three, potentially cut off for six weeks or more, here's a starting framework:

- Dry staples: rice, pasta, flour, oats, sugar, salt, cooking oil, tea, powdered or UHT milk.
- Tinned goods: meat, fish, beans, vegetables, fruit.
- Freezer: fill it before the wet — meat, bread, batch-cooked meals.
- Fuel: generator fuel, spare jerry cans, gas bottles.
- Medical: first aid kit restocked, any regular medications with extra buffer.

What's your water situation — tank or bore?

Tank water off the roof so the wet season actually fills the tanks up. That is the one thing that gets easier when the rain comes. Power is mains but it goes out in the big storms. I have a small generator that runs the freezer and the lights but not the aircon. I need to check the jerry cans and fill them up.

Good that the tanks are rain-fed. For the freezer — fill any empty space with bottles of water. They freeze solid, keep the freezer cold much longer during outages, and you can drink the water as they thaw. A full freezer can hold safe temperature for about 48 hours if you keep the door shut. Top up the generator fuel early — fuel deliveries can be unreliable once the wet really sets in.

Frozen water bottles, I have not done that before. That is a good idea. I will fill some up this week. The other thing is medications. I need to get those sorted in Darwin before the roads close. Cannot run out of anything important when you are stuck out here for weeks.

Most pharmacies can provide an extended supply if you explain the remote access situation. It's worth calling ahead to your pharmacy in Darwin so they have it ready when you go in. Give yourself more buffer than you think you'll need — if the wet runs long, you don't want to be counting pills.

I will call them this week. The other thing on my mind is school supplies for the kids. The school usually sends work home before the wet if they know the roads will close. I need to check with the teacher about that. The kids need exercise books and pencils and whatever else. Cannot have them sitting around for six weeks with nothing to do.

Following up with the teacher soon is a good idea — you don't want to be chasing it at the last minute. If the kids will be doing remote learning for weeks, having all the materials organised in advance saves a lot of frustration. You might also want some extra things to keep them occupied beyond schoolwork — books, cards, games. Six weeks is a long time for kids when they can't get out much.

They are not bad at keeping themselves busy but you are right, some extra books would be good. I will grab some in Jabiru. And mozzie gear. Wet season mosquitoes out here are relentless. We sleep on the veranda when the house is too hot and the mozzies come in thick after dark.

Mosquito nets, coils, and repellent — stock up more than you think you'll need. If the nets have any holes from last season, it's worth replacing them now. For the veranda, mosquito coils placed upwind of where you sleep can make a noticeable difference. And make sure the kids are covered — they're the ones who'll scratch bites raw.

I will check the nets this weekend and replace any that are worn. Alright, I think I have a proper list now. Rice, tins, flour, fuel, medications, freezer stocked, mozzie gear, school supplies, books for the kids. Big shop in Jabiru this week and medications from Darwin.

That's comprehensive. Getting it done early is the right approach — once the build-up really hits, the shelves in town thin out fast because everyone else is scrambling for the same things. One last thought: make sure your torch batteries and a battery-powered radio are sorted. When the power goes out and the phone service drops, a radio keeps you connected to weather updates.

Batteries and radio, yes. I always forget the radio until the power goes out and then I am looking for it in the dark. I will add it to the list. Thanks for helping me think through it all. Better to be ready now than scrambling when the first big rain comes through.
You've got a solid plan. Hope the wet season treats you and the kids well this year. Stay safe out there.
